Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent study routine, usually setting aside about 20 hours each week spread across several days. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through written materials and taking notes in my own words. I'd usually block out a few hours in the morning when I felt most focused, though I wasn't always strict about it. Honestly, some weeks were better than others depending on how motivated I felt.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. I tried to study at consistent times, but when assignments were due, I'd definitely cram more hours in during those days. When nothing pressing was coming up, I sometimes let my routine slip a bit because my motivation wasn't always there to push me forward.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if any assignment deadlines were approaching. I logged in pretty frequently—around 30 times over the course—mostly out of habit and wanting to stay on top of things. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any updates from peers since I interacted with quite a few of them through the platform.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging into the LMS just became part of my morning routine. I'd check it like I check my email, just to see what was new. It wasn't exciting or anything, but it felt necessary to stay organized. The mindfulness exercises I was doing helped me build some structure into my day, and checking the LMS fit into that.
Q5: The written course materials and reading assignments were definitely the most useful for me. Since I learn best through reading and writing, I really appreciated having detailed text resources I could work through at my own pace. I also found peer interactions helpful—being able to see what others were thinking helped me understand concepts from different angles.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums for posting, even though I read them sometimes. I just never felt compelled to add my own thoughts publicly—it felt like extra effort that wouldn't really help my grade. The videos I watched quite a bit, but I often skimmed through them rather than watching every minute carefully.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help me complete something that counted toward my grade, I was more likely to engage with it. Otherwise, with my motivation being what it is, I'd often just skip things that seemed optional or redundant.
Q8: Definitely. Required activities felt like obligations I had to check off, while optional ones felt like extras I could ignore without consequence. I rarely did optional activities unless I was genuinely curious or thought they might help with an assignment. My energy is limited, so I had to prioritize what actually mattered for passing.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and readings to my laptop so I could study offline. I also kept a notebook where I wrote summaries and key points in my own words—that's really how I learn best. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the LMS, just me and my notes.
Q10: There were many times when I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded materials without ever logging into the LMS. Sometimes I'd print things out and study at a coffee shop with no internet. The LMS would show nothing for those days, but I was definitely learning.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a reading or a set of lecture notes once, and then I'd work through them multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would only show that one download, but I might have spent five or six hours with that material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement got harder, usually when I felt overwhelmed or my motivation dipped. With my resilience being pretty low, small setbacks could throw me off for a few days. When multiple deadlines piled up or I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just disengage rather than push through.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. Some weeks I was just tired and couldn't bring myself to log in as often. When I had other things going on in life, the course took a back seat. My assignment completion rate wasn't great partly because of this—I'd run out of energy before finishing everything.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd open materials and scroll through them without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, technically watching a video or reading, but my mind was elsewhere. It looked like engagement on the surface, but I wasn't actually learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't get myself to start sometimes. Other times it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to begin. The mindfulness practice helped a bit, but it didn't solve everything.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become much more active on the LMS, downloading materials, checking requirements, and submitting work. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less. They created urgency that my natural motivation didn't provide.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though I sometimes took a while to process it. When I got positive feedback on my assignments—which scored pretty well—it encouraged me to keep going. But when feedback was critical, I'd sometimes avoid looking at it for a few days because I didn't want to feel discouraged.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which helped since I'm not great at self-directing when motivation is low. Having clear modules and deadlines kept me on track. But the structure also meant that if I fell behind, it was hard to catch up, which happened a few times.
Q19: Having all the readings available digitally and downloadable made engagement easier for me as a read-write learner. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or quick responses—I preferred working at my own pace. The discussion requirements felt awkward since I'm not naturally inclined to post publicly.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, downloading things, or trying to understand the platform without actually completing work. Maybe they're anxious about starting assignments or they get lost in the materials. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just clicking around without focus.
Q21: That actually sounds like me to some extent. I didn't post in discussions and my LMS activity was moderate, but I scored well on assignments because I did a lot of work offline. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but I was just engaging in ways the system couldn't track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like login frequency, time spent on videos, and submission timing. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply about the material. They also miss all the offline studying, note-taking, and reflection that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, making connections, and trying to apply what I'm learning. For me, it's less about how often I log in and more about whether I'm genuinely processing the content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with my handwritten notes, or the mental effort I put into understanding difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practice that helped me manage stress and stay somewhat on track. And it definitely wouldn't show the internal struggle with motivation that shaped how I approached everything.
